section.title: Continuity
section.p: ended with a mismatch. The nearby values of \frac{x^2y}{x^2+y^2} wanted to approach 0 at the origin, but we could still define the value at the origin to be 17. Limits notice what happens nearby. Continuity asks whether the function agrees with its own nearby behavior.

subsection.title: Continuity as nearby inputs give nearby outputs
subsection.p: A temperature field should not usually jump suddenly. If you move a few centimeters across a room, the temperature should change only a little. A formula can express that kind of steadiness.
The definition contains three requirements at once:

li.p: \displaystyle \lim_{\mathbf x\to\mathbf a}F(\mathbf x) must equal F(\mathbf a).

subsection.p: If any one of these fails, continuity fails.

statement.p: Define f(x,y)=\frac{x^2+y^2}{\sqrt{x^2+y^2}},\qquad (x,y)\ne(0,0). For (x,y)\ne(0,0), f(x,y)=\sqrt{x^2+y^2}. Thus \lim_{(x,y)\to(0,0)} f(x,y)=0. But the original formula is not defined at (0,0). So f is not continuous at the origin.
If we define f(0,0)=0, then the repaired function becomes continuous at the origin. The discontinuity was only a missing value.

example.title: A discontinuity that cannot be repaired

statement.p: Let h(x,y)=\frac{xy}{x^2+y^2},\qquad (x,y)\ne(0,0). Along the x-axis, h(x,0)=0. Along the line y=x, h(x,x)=\frac{x^2}{2x^2}=\frac12. The limit at the origin does not exist. No choice of h(0,0) can make the function continuous there.
A missing value can be repaired only when the limit exists.

subsection.p: For vector-valued functions, continuity is componentwise, just as limits are.

statement.p: Let F:D\subseteq\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R^m be written as F(\mathbf x)=\bigl(F_1(\mathbf x),F_2(\mathbf x),\ldots,F_m(\mathbf x)\bigr). Then F is continuous at \mathbf a if and only if each component function F_i:D\to\mathbb R is continuous at \mathbf a.

proof.p: This follows directly from the componentwise limit theorem in . The vector limit \lim_{\mathbf x\to\mathbf a}F(\mathbf x)=F(\mathbf a) holds exactly when every component limit satisfies \lim_{\mathbf x\to\mathbf a}F_i(\mathbf x)=F_i(\mathbf a).

statement.p: The swirling field \mathbf W(x,y)=(-y,x) is continuous everywhere in the plane. Its components -y\qquad\text{and}\qquad x are continuous functions.

The field \mathbf V(x,y)=\frac{1}{x^2+y^2}(-y,x) is continuous on its domain \mathbb R^2\setminus\{(0,0)\}. It is not continuous at the origin because it is not even defined there. The missing point is not a small detail; it will matter later when we study circulation around holes.

subsection.title: Algebra of continuous functions
subsection.p: Most continuous functions are built from simpler continuous functions.

For example, suppose the pressure in a tank is modeled by p(x,y,z)=\frac{100+2z}{1+x^2+y^2}. The numerator 100+2z is continuous. The denominator 1+x^2+y^2 is continuous and never equals 0. So the quotient behaves continuously everywhere in space.
This example is so common that we turn it into a theorem.

statement.p: Suppose f,g:D\subseteq\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R are continuous at \mathbf a\in D, and let c be a constant. Then the following functions are continuous at \mathbf a: f+g,\qquad f-g,\qquad cf,\qquad fg. If g(\mathbf a)\ne0, then \frac{f}{g} is also continuous at \mathbf a.

proof.p: The limit laws from give \lim_{\mathbf x\to\mathbf a}(f+g)(\mathbf x)=f(\mathbf a)+g(\mathbf a), and similarly for differences, constant multiples, and products.

For quotients, the limit law for quotients applies when the limiting denominator is not zero: \lim_{\mathbf x\to\mathbf a}g(\mathbf x)=g(\mathbf a)\ne0. Thus \lim_{\mathbf x\to\mathbf a}\frac{f(\mathbf x)}{g(\mathbf x)}=\frac{f(\mathbf a)}{g(\mathbf a)}.

subsection.p: We required g(\mathbf a)\ne0 for a reason.

statement.p: Let f(x,y)=1,\qquad g(x,y)=x^2+y^2. Both functions are continuous everywhere. But \frac{f(x,y)}{g(x,y)}=\frac{1}{x^2+y^2} is not continuous at the origin. It is not even defined there, and its values become unbounded as (x,y)\to(0,0).
So a quotient of continuous functions is continuous only where the denominator is not zero.

example.title: A formula continuous on its natural domain

statement.p: The square root requires 4-x^2-y^2\ge0, so x^2+y^2\le4. The denominator requires 1+xy\ne0. Therefore q is continuous on \{(x,y):x^2+y^2\le4,\ xy\ne -1\}.

subsection.p: This gives a practical habit:
subsection.md: \text{A formula made from continuous pieces is continuous wherever it is allowed to be.}
subsection.p: The suspicious points are the places where the formula is not allowed: zero denominators, square roots of negative numbers, logarithms of nonpositive numbers, and special piecewise definitions.

subsection.title: Continuity of compositions
subsection.p: A function can feed into another function.

For example, the height of a dome over a circular floor is h(x,y)=\sqrt{16-x^2-y^2}. In polar coordinates, x=r\cos\theta,\qquad y=r\sin\theta. Substituting gives
h(r\cos\theta,r\sin\theta) \amp= \sqrt{16-r^2\cos^2\theta-r^2\sin^2\theta} \amp= \sqrt{16-r^2}.
The coordinate map \Phi(r,\theta)=(r\cos\theta,r\sin\theta) is continuous. The height function h is continuous on its disk. Their composition h\circ \Phi is continuous wherever 16-r^2\ge0.

statement.p: Let F:D\subseteq\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R^m be continuous at \mathbf a, and let G:E\subseteq\mathbb R^m\to\mathbb R^k be continuous at F(\mathbf a). If F(D)\subseteq E, then the composition G\circ F is continuous at \mathbf a.

proof.p: Since F is continuous at \mathbf a, F(\mathbf x)\to F(\mathbf a)\qquad\text{as}\qquad\mathbf x\to\mathbf a. Since G is continuous at F(\mathbf a), G(\mathbf y)\to G(F(\mathbf a))\qquad\text{as}\qquad\mathbf y\to F(\mathbf a). Taking \mathbf y=F(\mathbf x), we get G(F(\mathbf x))\to G(F(\mathbf a)). So G\circ F is continuous at \mathbf a.

subsection.p: The continuity hypotheses are real. If either piece has a jump in the wrong place, the composition can have a jump.

example.title: A discontinuous outer function can break the composition
A discontinuous inner function can also break the composition

subsection.title: Continuous functions on closed and bounded regions
subsection.p: Single-variable calculus has a comforting theorem: a continuous function on a closed interval [a,b] has a maximum and a minimum. Multivariable calculus has the same idea, but the interval becomes a closed and bounded region.
First, an example.
Let H(x,y)=120-x^2-4y^2 on the rectangle D=\{(x,y):0\le x\le3,\ 0\le y\le2\}. This is the height function from our contour-map hill, restricted to a rectangular park.
The function is largest at (0,0), where H(0,0)=120. It is smallest at (3,2), where H(3,2)=120-9-16=95. So this hill has both a highest point and a lowest point on the park.
The rectangle matters. It contains its edges, and it does not stretch to infinity.

statement.p: A set D\subseteq\mathbb R^n is bounded if it fits inside some large ball. That is, there is a number M such that \|\mathbf x\|\le M for every \mathbf x\in D.
A set D\subseteq\mathbb R^n is closed if it contains all of its boundary points.
In this book, the most important examples are regions described by non-strict inequalities, such as x^2+y^2\le 9 or 0\le x\le 3,\qquad 0\le y\le 2.

subsection.p: The disk x^2+y^2\le1 is closed and bounded. The open disk x^2+y^2\lt 1 is bounded but not closed, because it leaves out its boundary circle. The whole plane \mathbb R^2 is closed but not bounded.

theorem.title: Extreme Value Theorem in several variables

statement.p: Let f:D\subseteq\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R be continuous. If D is closed and bounded, then f attains an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum on D.
That means there are points \mathbf p,\mathbf q\in D such that f(\mathbf p)\le f(\mathbf x)\le f(\mathbf q) for every \mathbf x\in D.

proof.title: Idea of the proof
proof.p: A closed and bounded region has no missing edge point and no escape to infinity. A continuous function cannot jump over a value or shoot upward without nearby values following it. These two facts together force the highest and lowest values to be reached.

The full proof belongs to a later analysis course. For this course, the theorem is a tool: on a closed and bounded region, a continuous function cannot merely approach its maximum or minimum. It must attain them.

subsection.p: Each hypothesis matters.

statement.p: The function f(x,y)=x is continuous on all of \mathbb R^2. The domain \mathbb R^2 is closed, but it is not bounded.
There is no maximum, because x can be made as large as we like. There is no minimum, because x can be made as negative as we like.
The function f(x,y)=x^2+y^2 is continuous on the open disk D=\{(x,y):x^2+y^2\lt 1\}. The domain is bounded, but it is not closed.
The values satisfy 0\le f(x,y)\lt 1. The function has a minimum at (0,0), but it has no maximum. The value 1 is approached near the boundary circle, but the boundary circle is not included.
As (x,y)\to(0,0), the values \frac{1}{x^2+y^2} become arbitrarily large. So u has no maximum on D. Closed and bounded domains do not protect us from discontinuities.

subsection.p: Continuity is a powerful promise, but it is also easy to assume too quickly. A formula may look harmless from a few straight paths and still hide a thin ridge, a missing point, or a sudden jump. Before we move on to slopes in many directions, it is worth collecting the main traps in one place.
